Capacity Expansion and Time-Sequential Dispatch Prototype v1.25

Browser-only learning model. Full-year 8,760-hour framing. No local Python required. Open-source under GPL-3.0-or-later, with embedded PLEXOS-World demand-shape attribution.

Ready

Inputs

How to: embedded mini-grid case example

Purpose. This tab explains the workflow for the preloaded mini-grid case. The case is loaded automatically when the file opens. Use Run optimisation on the Dashboard to solve it, or use the reload button below to restore the embedded case after edits.
1. Dashboard framing

The Dashboard sets the model horizon, start date, Economic Investment Horizon, Cost of Capital / Target IRR, tax rate, currency, carbon price, unserved-energy penalty, project notes and save/load controls. The preloaded case is an 8,760-hour 2019 local-year case with a 20-year economic investment horizon, 8% real Cost of Capital / Target IRR, 30% tax rate, USD currency, 0 $/tCO2 carbon price and 50,000 $/MWh unserved-energy penalty. Maximum hourly and annual unserved-energy caps are left blank in the embedded setup.

The visible workflow is now year-long and time-sequential by design. The old sample-profile controls, full-year toggle and annual-scaling selector are not part of the Dashboard. A hidden internal scaling field remains set to as_entered for save/load compatibility.

The Dashboard diagram is a conceptual nodal abstraction. In two-node mode it shows the System Node, Node 2 and directed transmission link, while still keeping candidate capacity, storage, dumping and unserved energy visible. It is not an alternating-current power-flow model.

v1.10 embedded setup. System Node holds all solar, wind, firm generation and storage candidates. Node 2 receives 100% of demand. Link 1 is a directed System Node to Node 2 transmission asset with 10 MW existing capacity, 250 MW maximum build and 1% loss. User System starts with a 110 MW Link 1 add-on for fixed-dispatch testing.

2. Demand framing in Grid Load Builder

The preloaded demand case is a theoretical full-year grid load with 8,760 hourly intervals. It totals 876,000 MWh/year, equivalent to 100 MW average demand.

Load segmentShareAverage MWAnnual MWhRepresentation
Industrial50%50438,000Grid Load Builder component using the industrial archetype
Residential20%20175,200Grid Load Builder component using the residential archetype
Commercial30%30262,800Grid Load Builder component using the commercial archetype
Total grid load100%100876,000Active model demand profile

Use Grid Load Builder to upload a reference demand profile, select and scale a PLEXOS-World proxy profile, build block and seasonal loads, or combine these sources through Demand Stack. Demand upload does not belong in Resource profiles.

3. Renewable resource framing

Resource profiles are framed at latitude -31, longitude 122. The Resource profiles tab provides the coordinate picker, Renewables.ninja CSV links and the renewable profile upload library.

ProfileRowsAverage capacity factorMaximum capacity factorSource treatment
Solar PV8,76016.66%77.90%Renewables.ninja CSV, electricity column, local-year aligned
Wind8,76037.05%98.70%Renewables.ninja CSV, electricity column, local-year aligned
4. Technology Candidates structure

Technology Candidates are organised into four user-facing technology classes. Each candidate has minimum and maximum build limits. Min build MW is a lower bound on new capacity selected by the optimiser. Existing MW is pre-existing capacity. Tech. Life is used in annualised cost calculations. The embedded v1.10 setup assigns all supply and storage candidates to System Node, while Node 2 carries the demand.

Technology classCandidatePreloaded build rangeKey cost and performance assumptionsNode
RenewablesSolar 110 to 150 MWCapex 600 $/kW, fixed operating cost 18 $/kW-year, variable operating cost 0 $/MWh, Tech. Life 25 yearsSystem Node
RenewablesWind 115 to 150 MWCapex 1,800 $/kW, fixed operating cost 36 $/kW-year, Tech. Life 25 yearsSystem Node
Firm GeneratorsCombined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT)25 to 50 MWCapex 2,500 $/kW, fixed operating cost 25 $/kW-year, variable cost 101.40 $/MWh, availability 85%, emissions 0.45 tCO2/MWhSystem Node
Firm GeneratorsOpen Cycle Gas Turbine (OCGT)0 to 150 MWCapex 625 $/kW, fixed operating cost 6 $/kW-year, variable cost 159 $/MWh, availability 95%, emissions 0.625 tCO2/MWhSystem Node
Energy Storage SystemsBattery Energy Storage System (BESS)0 to 50 MW / 300 MWhFixed operating cost 8 $/kW-year, Tech. Life 12 years, round-trip efficiency 88%System Node
Energy Storage SystemsPumped Hydro10 to 20 MW / 80 MWhFixed operating cost 8 $/kW-year, Tech. Life 50 years, round-trip efficiency 67%System Node
External Grid InterfaceExternal import0 MWImport price 220 $/MWh and emissions 0.50 tCO2/MWh are retained, but import is disabled by the 0 MW limitAggregate interface
5. Economic investment horizon and asset recovery

The system-cost objective uses the Dashboard Economic Investment Horizon, preset to 20 years. If a candidate's Tech. Life is longer than the economic horizon, capital is recovered over the economic horizon. If Tech. Life is shorter, the model adds a discounted full or partial refurbishment or replacement provision before the end of the economic horizon.

This is a screening-grade annualisation convention. It is useful for comparing technologies with different useful lives on a consistent planning basis, but it is not a replacement for a full project-finance cash-flow model.

6. Recommended workflow
  1. Confirm Dashboard economics, especially Economic Investment Horizon, Cost of Capital / Target IRR, tax rate and unserved-energy penalty.
  2. Use Grid Load Builder to inspect or rebuild the demand profile, then apply the framed demand to the model.
  3. Use Resource profiles to confirm the coordinate frame and renewable profile library.
  4. Use Network to review the two-node setup, demand allocation and Link 1 directed transfer assumptions.
  5. Use Technology Candidates to review Renewables, Firm Generators, Energy Storage Systems and External Grid Interface settings, including Min build MW, Max build MW, Existing MW, assigned node and Tech. Life.
  6. Use Constraints & Settings only for reliability, emissions, renewable-share and solver controls.
  7. Run the optimisation from Dashboard.
  8. Review the Decision Summary, binding constraints, commercial-reality panel, stress-test layer, operational charts, cost bridge and energy-balance audit. Long-running solve status is also mirrored in the top header status bar.
  9. Use User System to compare the optimised build against a fixed candidate-level installed system. Select User-entered system in the Results plot selector to solve and plot it directly.
Decision-use boundary. This is a transparent linear screening model. It excludes unit commitment, integer build blocks, alternating-current power flow, security constraints and stochastic weather-year testing.
Save, restore and share setup

Save the complete working setup as a portable JSON file. This includes system settings, candidate assets, loaded demand and renewable profiles, load-builder components, map/resource assumptions and optional solved results. Another user can open the same HTML file, load this JSON, and rerun or inspect the case.

No saved setup loaded in this session.
Ready. Year-long time-sequential capacity expansion framing is active. Build demand through Grid Load Builder or load a saved setup, then run optimisation.
Dashboard abstraction: node-link diagramConfigured system

Conceptual nodal view. In one-node mode the existing hub-and-spoke view is retained. In multi-node mode the diagram uses node cards and directed link arrows, with technology candidates and storage grouped inside their assigned node. Drag node cards to arrange the diagram for readability. This is still a linear transport abstraction, not an alternating-current power-flow model.

Hover or click a hub-and-spoke box to view configured limits, optimised installed capacity and user-system framing.
This is a screening and teaching model. It excludes unit commitment, alternating-current power flow, security constraints and integer build blocks.
Publication, data provenance and license

Software license: GNU General Public License v3.0 or later (GPL-3.0-or-later). Users may use, share and modify the tool. If they distribute modified versions, the modified source should remain available under the same license.

PLEXOS-World demand data attribution: the embedded demand library is a compressed reconstruction derived from PLEXOS-World 2015, Harvard Dataverse, DOI 10.7910/DVN/CBYXBY. The specific demand-file reference used during development was doi:10.7910/DVN/CBYXBY/JCQPTE.

Recommended citation: Brinkerink, M., and Deane, P. (2020). PLEXOS-World 2015. Harvard Dataverse. Also cite the related article, Building and Calibrating a Country-Level Detailed Global Electricity Model Based on Public Data, when using the embedded demand profiles in published or shared analysis.

Netlify note: publish the generic app only. Do not publish saved setup JSON files containing confidential locations, demand traces, costs, capacities or commercial assumptions.

Resource coordinate frame

Use this section to pick a location and generate one-year solar and wind CSV download links from Renewables.ninja. Click or drag the map marker to set the target coordinate, download the PV and wind CSV files, then upload those files below. This section is for renewable resource data only.

Map marker sets the latitude and longitude used for solar and wind CSV downloads.

Renewables.ninja point downloads are framed as a single calendar-year request. The hidden legacy date fields are retained for save/load compatibility and are generated from the selected Reference Calendar Year.

Solar assumptions

Wind assumptions

Dropdown uses the built-in v1.03 Renewables.ninja preset list. Saved custom turbine values are preserved if loaded.
Recommended workflow: choose the location and assumptions above, then use the CSV buttons below. If the browser opens the CSV instead of downloading it, save the page as a .csv file and upload it here.
No Renewables.ninja resource profiles loaded yet.

Resource CSV uploads

Upload solar and wind capacity-factor files. Files can be raw capacity factors from 0 to 1, percentages from 0 to 100, or native Renewables.ninja CSV exports. For Renewables.ninja files, the parser uses the electricity column, not wind_speed, irradiance or temperature.

Loaded renewable profile library

Upload one or more PV and wind files. Each file is stored as a named profile that can be assigned to a specific solar or wind candidate under Technology Candidates. The most recently loaded profile also becomes the current default profile.

ClassNameRowsAverage CFMax CFSource
Resource profile preview

Chart preview shows the first 168 hours of solar and wind capacity factors.

Reference demand CSV upload

Upload up to 8,760 hourly MW values as a reference grid-load profile. This can be previewed, applied directly to the model, or combined with a selected PLEXOS-World demand block and/or enabled Load Builder blocks through Demand Stack below.

No reference demand CSV loaded. The embedded case uses the active demand profile created from the Grid Load Builder components.

PLEXOS-World demand profile library

Use this to load a country or regional proxy demand shape from the embedded PLEXOS-World 2015 demand file, convert it from UTC into a local-year profile, then rescale it to the project size. The working convention is that the final model profile starts at 1 Jan 00:00 local time, aligned with the local-time treatment of Renewables.ninja exports.

Time alignment not yet calculated.
Optional: replace embedded library with a downloaded PLEXOS file
Embedded PLEXOS-World demand library is ready.

Load builder: block and seasonal loads

Build a local demand shape by combining load components. Each component has a 24-hour archetype and a 12-month seasonal scalar. The 24-hour shape is normalised to daily average 1.00, so the component's average MW remains intuitive.

UseNameArchetypeAverage MWPeak MWAnnual MWh
24-hour shape grid for selected component

Values are relative hourly scalars. They are normalised internally to daily average 1.00.

12-month seasonal scalar grid for selected component

Enter monthly multipliers. Example: Jan-Mar at 2.00, Apr-Jun at 0.70, other months at 1.00.

Load builder not initialised.

Demand stack: combine reference, PLEXOS and load-builder demand

Use this to combine an uploaded reference demand profile, a selected PLEXOS proxy profile and/or project-specific load-builder blocks. The combined profile starts at 1 Jan 00:00 local time.

Demand stack not calculated yet.
Operational rule: PLEXOS-World can provide the base demand shape and the Load Builder can add project-specific loads. The scale remains a modelling assumption unless the user enters a peak, average or annual demand. The default applies a circular UTC-to-local-year shift so hour 0 means 1 Jan 00:00 local time. Electricity Maps coverage is used for zone-context alignment, not as a demand feed in this browser-only prototype.

Network

v1.13 multi-node screening formulation. The embedded two-node setup auto-loads at browser startup; embedded case payload remains compacted. One-node cases remain on the existing formulation. With two to four nodes, enabled directed transmission assets become linear transport links with capacity, losses and costs. v1.13 adds a node filter for the Supply-demand stack chart. Hotfix: node filter is re-applied and charts are re-rendered after loading saved setup + results JSON.
Nodes
1
Demand allocation
100%
Transmission assets
0

Nodes / regions

Default one-node mode uses System Node. Add one more node to activate the v1.06 two-node transport formulation. Demand is allocated to nodes using the percentages below.

Node nameNode typeDemand allocation %Diagram XDiagram YNotes
Demand allocation is metadata only in v1.05.

Transmission assets active in v1.13 multi-node mode

Directed transfer limits, losses and costs become active when two nodes are framed. Links can be directed or bidirectional. Bidirectional convenience mode internally creates forward and reverse non-negative flow variables with shared capacity and shared build cost.

EnabledAsset nameFrom nodeTo nodeExisting MWMax build MWLoss %Notes
Transmission is active in two-to-four-node mode. Directed links use one non-negative flow direction. Bidirectional links use a shared capacity where forward flow plus reverse flow cannot exceed total transfer capacity in an hour. In one-node mode this table remains saved metadata and the original single-node solve path is preserved.
v1.06 compatibility rule: one-node cases remain unchanged. Two-node cases use directed transport links; no alternating-current power flow or nodal pricing is implied.

Candidate assets

Add multiple candidates within each technology class. Solar and wind candidates sit under Renewables and can be assigned different uploaded resource profiles. The optimiser can build each candidate independently, subject to minimum and maximum build limits.

Capital cost is annualised against the Dashboard Economic Investment Horizon. If Tech. Life is longer than the economic horizon, capital is recovered within the economic horizon. If Tech. Life is shorter, the model adds a discounted full or partial refurbishment provision before the end of the economic horizon.

Technology Class: Renewables profile selectable by candidate

Solar candidates

Solar candidate fields are split across technical/resource settings and economics/build limits.

Wind candidates

Wind candidate fields are split across technical/resource settings and economics/build limits.

Technology Class: Firm Generators

Firm generator candidate fields are split across operating parameters and economics/build limits.

Technology Class: Energy Storage Systems

Energy storage candidate fields are split across technical settings and economics/build limits.

External Grid Interface

Candidate detail is shown in the capacity table after optimisation. For firm generators, VOM $/MWh is calculated as fuel cost $/GJ x (3.6 / thermal efficiency fraction) + non-fuel VOM $/MWh. Loss is entered in percentage points: 0.12 means 0.12%, not 12%. Negative values are allowed as a modelling shorthand for hub-and-spoke representations where a generator is credited with a negative marginal loss factor at the supply-demand node. Parasitic MW/MW adds a constant load for every MW of installed capacity. Min build MW is a lower bound on new capacity built by the optimiser, not existing capacity. Aggregate charts still group solar, wind, firm and energy storage for readability. Candidate-level profile choice, build limits and capacity detail are retained in the capacity table and setup export.

User-entered installed system

Use this to test a specified fixed system configuration. The model will run dispatch only: no new capacity is added. The installed capacity fields below are generated from the candidates framed in Technology Candidates, so the user can nominate exactly which solar, wind, firm generation and Energy Storage System candidate is installed.

Candidate-level installed capacityNo user-system capacities framed

These fields are dispatch inputs, not expansion limits. Candidate max-build values from Technology Candidates are shown for context, but the user-entered system can deliberately test a fixed configuration outside those bounds.

ClassCandidateTechnology frameUser installed MWUser installed MWhDispatch interpretation
Comparison basis: the optimiser still chooses capacity on an incremental basis, but reported system cost includes annualised fixed cost for total installed capacity. This makes an optimal greenfield solution and a candidate-level user-entered greenfield solution comparable.

Comparison summary

Controlled constraints

Optimisation settings

0 means no explicit time limit.
When checked, final storage state must equal the starting state through the year wrap.
Supply-share constraints are framed against served demand at the supply-demand node, including technology parasitic loads. Hourly shortfall is implemented as an upper bound on unserved load in every hour. Loss factors are percentage-point adjustments to delivered supply at the node; negative values are allowed to represent a simplified negative marginal loss factor. Parasitic load adds a constant MW load per MW of installed capacity.

Generated LP preview

Embedded developer documentation and handover

Purpose. This documentation makes the HTML self-explanatory in a new development session. It describes the current v1.10 structure, data flow, formulation and known limits so the next maintainer can patch the file without rebuilding it from scratch.
0. Publication, licensing and data attribution
Software license. This tool is released as free software under the GNU General Public License v3.0 or later, using SPDX identifier GPL-3.0-or-later.
Repository packaging. For Netlify or GitHub publishing, ship at least index.html, LICENSE.txt and README.md. The HTML file carries an embedded SPDX notice, but the separate licence file should remain in the package.
PLEXOS-World demand source. The embedded PLEXOS demand library is a compressed reconstruction of hourly demand traces derived from PLEXOS-World 2015: Brinkerink, M., and Deane, P. (2020). PLEXOS-World 2015. Harvard Dataverse. DOI: 10.7910/DVN/CBYXBY. The development file reference was doi:10.7910/DVN/CBYXBY/JCQPTE.
Related paper. When embedded PLEXOS demand shapes are material to analysis, cite Brinkerink, M., Gallachoir, B. O., and Deane, P. (2021), Building and Calibrating a Country-Level Detailed Global Electricity Model Based on Public Data, Energy Strategy Reviews.
Third-party services and libraries. Leaflet, map tiles, HiGHS, Renewables.ninja, Harvard Dataverse, Electricity Maps and browser dependencies retain their own licences, terms and trademarks.
Confidentiality warning. The published app should be generic. Saved setup JSON files may contain sensitive demand, cost, capacity, location and commercial assumptions.
1. Current product intent and v1.06 scope
v1.06 position. The app shell is v1.06. It keeps the v1.04 pre-solve validation layer and adds a one-node-compatible Network section with nodes, staged transmission assets and candidate node assignment. The optimiser remains the v1.04 single-node linear formulation. The preloaded case is the supplied Mini-grid case example JSON setup, compacted for browser loading.

The app is a single-file, browser-only capacity expansion and dispatch prototype for mini-grids, remote systems, industrial hubs, local energy systems and partially grid-connected supply-demand problems.

The model solves a linear optimisation problem over up to 8,760 hourly intervals. It chooses new capacity for candidate technologies and dispatches them hour by hour to minimise annualised system cost while satisfying demand, storage, import, emissions, reliability and policy constraints.

It is not a production-grade market model or electrical network-planning platform. It does not include unit commitment, integer build blocks, alternating-current power flow, contingency analysis, voltage constraints, inertia, frequency security, detailed hydro scheduling, forced-outage sampling or stochastic weather-year ensembles.

2. Packaging, dependencies and operating model
  • File structure: one portable index.html containing interface, styles, embedded data and JavaScript model logic.
  • v1.06 network compatibility: the Network section is saved, restored and passed into the two-node directed transport linear programme when two nodes are active.
  • Package files: index.html, LICENSE.txt and README.md.
  • No local Python: users should not need Python or a local server.
  • Solver: HiGHS JavaScript and WebAssembly are loaded by the browser. The optimisation problem is built as linear-programme text and solved by HiGHS.
  • Maps: Leaflet provides resource-location context. Map tiles require internet access unless vendored later.
  • Charts: custom Canvas rendering is used for operational plots.
  • Persistence: browser session state plus portable setup JSON save/load.
  • Preloaded case: the embedded JSON is a v0.51 saved setup with candidate-level settings. Its demand and renewable profile arrays are compacted in the HTML preload and expanded in-browser before the setup is restored.
  • Renewables.ninja: the app generates direct CSV download links. It does not embed API tokens or rely on authenticated browser-side fetches.
3. User interface map by tab

How to

  • Explains the preloaded case and the recommended user workflow.

Dashboard

  • Sets model horizon, start date, Economic Investment Horizon, Cost of Capital / Target IRR, tax rate, currency, carbon price, unserved-energy penalty, project notes and save/load settings.
  • Runs the optimisation and shows the conceptual hub-and-spoke single-node abstraction.
  • The legacy sample-profile buttons and annual-scaling selector are intentionally not visible. A hidden scaling field remains for backwards compatibility.

Grid Load Builder

  • Loads a reference demand CSV up to 8,760 hourly MW values.
  • Provides the embedded PLEXOS-World demand profile library, searchable by country, region, code or Electricity Maps hint.
  • Scales PLEXOS profiles by peak MW, average MW or annual MWh and can convert UTC profiles to local-year order.
  • Builds synthetic block and seasonal loads through editable components.
  • Combines uploaded reference demand, selected PLEXOS demand and enabled Load Builder components through Demand Stack.

Resource profiles

  • Provides the Leaflet coordinate picker and Renewables.ninja CSV download-link generation.
  • Frames renewable-resource assumptions: dataset, Reference Calendar Year, photovoltaic loss, tracking, tilt, azimuth, wind hub height and turbine type.
  • Reference Calendar Year generates hidden date_from and date_to API parameters for the selected calendar year. Manual date fields are no longer exposed in the UI.
  • The turbine selector is a dropdown seeded with Renewables.ninja wind-turbine model names used in its published examples and update notes; saved custom values remain recoverable through setup JSON.
  • Uploads one or more solar and wind capacity-factor or Renewables.ninja CSV files.
  • Maintains a renewable profile library so each solar and wind candidate can select a different profile.

Network

  • Adds a one-node-compatible Network section with Nodes / regions and staged Transmission assets tables.
  • Stores node name, type, demand allocation percentage, notes and diagram X/Y position.
  • Frames directed transmission assets that become active in the two-node transport formulation.

Technology Candidates

  • Groups candidates under Technology Class: Renewables, Technology Class: Firm Generators, Technology Class: Energy Storage Systems and External Grid Interface.
  • Splits candidate input fields across two rows for readability.
  • Adds Min build MW lower bounds, keeps Max build MW upper bounds and renames Life to Tech. Life.
  • Calculates firm-generator variable operating cost from fuel cost, thermal efficiency and non-fuel variable operating cost.
  • Adds a node assignment selector to each candidate. In v1.05 this is saved metadata only; it is not used by the optimiser.

Constraints & Settings

  • Sets minimum annual solar, wind and combined renewable served-demand shares.
  • Sets maximum hourly unserved power, maximum annual unserved energy and maximum annual emissions.
  • Sets curtailment penalty, solver time limit, presolve and storage state-of-charge treatment.

User System

  • Runs dispatch for a fixed, candidate-level user-entered system without new capacity build. Selecting User-entered system in the Results plot selector now triggers a dispatch solve if no user-system result exists and plots the result only after an optimal dispatch is returned.
  • Compares user-entered capacity and dispatch economics with the optimised system.

Results

  • Shows KPI tiles, capacity and energy summaries, system-wide Levelised Cost of Electricity, operational charts, dispatch export, capacity tables and energy-balance audit.
4. Demand systems in detail

The active model demand is stored in state.profiles.demand. The preloaded case carries an explicit 8,760-hour demand profile and the corresponding Load Builder components.

Reference demand upload

Reference demand CSV upload sits in Grid Load Builder. The uploaded profile is stored separately from the active model demand, can be previewed, can be applied directly, and can be included in Demand Stack.

PLEXOS-World library

The embedded PLEXOS library contains country and regional proxy demand shapes. The default workflow converts UTC source traces into local-year order so hour 0 means 1 Jan 00:00 local time. Users can upload the original PLEXOS demand file if exact raw traces are required.

Load Builder

Load Builder creates project-specific components from archetypes. Components have a name, enable flag, average MW scale, editable 24-hour shape and editable 12-month seasonal scalars. The 24-hour shape is normalised to daily average 1.00. Monthly scalars can also be normalised to annual weighted average 1.00.

Demand Stack

combined_demand[t] = uploaded_reference_demand[t] + selected_PLEXOS_profile[t] + sum(enabled_load_builder_components[t])

The combined demand can be previewed, applied and exported. The Operational view includes a pre-optimisation demand framing chart.

Implementation rule: do not re-render the Load Builder table on every keystroke. Update state on input, and re-render only on structural edits or change.
5. Profile parsing and renewable resource treatment
  • Demand CSV values are interpreted as MW.
  • Solar and wind files can be capacity factors from 0 to 1, percentages from 0 to 100, or native Renewables.ninja CSV exports.
  • For Renewables.ninja files, use the electricity column. Do not accidentally use wind speed, irradiance or temperature columns.
  • If Renewables.ninja metadata contains capacity, electricity output is converted to capacity factor using electricity / capacity.
  • Capacity factors are clamped after parsing.
  • Uploaded renewable files are added to state.resourceProfiles.solar or state.resourceProfiles.wind and can be selected per candidate.
6. Technology fields and modelling interpretation

Renewables

  • Solar and wind candidates use a selected resource profile, resource scalar, loss percentage-point adjustment, parasitic MW per MW, capex, fixed operating cost, variable operating cost, Tech. Life, Min build MW, Max build MW and Existing MW.
  • Min build MW is a lower bound on new build. It can be used to force a minimum capacity outcome in the problem formulation.

Firm Generators

  • Firm generators use capex, fixed operating cost, fuel cost, thermal efficiency, non-fuel variable operating cost, calculated variable operating cost, Tech. Life, availability, emissions, loss, parasitic load, Min build MW, Max build MW and Existing MW.
  • Calculated variable operating cost is fuel cost $/GJ x (3.6 / efficiency fraction) + non-fuel VOM $/MWh.

Energy Storage Systems

  • Storage has separate power and energy decision variables.
  • v1.12 storage-location audit: in multi-node cases the Results diagnostics now report storage power, energy, charge, discharge, net discharge and congestion interaction by node so users can test whether storage is more valuable at the supply node or the demand node.
  • Round-trip efficiency is split into symmetric charge and discharge efficiency in the storage equation.
  • Min build MW applies to storage power capacity. Energy capacity remains bounded by maximum MWh.
  • Discharge loss applies to delivered discharge at the supply-demand node. Parasitic MW/MW adds auxiliary load per MW of installed power.

External Grid Interface

  • The External Grid Interface is a capped import option with price, emissions intensity and maximum import MW.
  • The preloaded case sets the import cap to 0 MW, so the interface is retained but disabled.
Negative loss values. Negative loss remains allowed as a shorthand for a simplified negative marginal loss factor in a hub-and-spoke abstraction. Extreme values should be treated with caution.
7. Economic Investment Horizon and capital recovery

The Dashboard Economic Investment Horizon, preset to 20 years, is the cost-minimisation basis for annualised system cost.

  • If Tech. Life is longer than the economic horizon, the capital recovery factor uses the economic horizon so capital is recovered within the planning period.
  • If Tech. Life is shorter than the economic horizon, the model adds an annualised discounted provision for full or partial refurbishment or replacement before the end of the horizon.
  • Fixed operating cost is added annually on installed capacity.
  • For storage, power capex and energy capex are annualised separately.

This is a transparent screening convention. It aligns technologies with different lives for least-cost comparison, but does not replace tax, straight-line depreciation, financing, salvage-value or major-maintenance modelling in a full investment case. The Results finance panel adds a simplified system-wide P&L and annual discounted cash-flow bridge, but it remains screening-grade.

8. Hub-and-spoke abstraction and hourly dispatch sequence

The Dashboard visual is a conceptual map of the optimisation problem. In one-node mode the hub is the supply-demand node. In multi-node mode the view changes to rectangular node cards with directed transmission arrows. Renewables, firm generators and Energy Storage Systems are grouped inside the node to which they are assigned. Link labels show existing MW, built MW after solve, total MW, loss percentage and binding hours. Hovering or clicking a node or link discloses configured limits, optimised installed capacity and user-system framed capacity where available.

Hourly dispatch is solved as one linear programme, not as a rule-based loop. For interpretation, available supply and imports are brought to the node, demand and parasitic loads are served, storage charges from surplus or discharges into shortfall subject to power and state-of-charge limits, and any residual surplus is dumped while residual shortfall is unserved energy.

9. Optimisation formulation

The objective is annualised total system cost over the Economic Investment Horizon, including capacity recovery, fixed operating cost, variable operating cost, External Grid Interface cost, carbon cost, unserved-energy penalty and optional curtailment penalty.

minimise:
  annualised capacity and fixed cost
+ variable generation and storage throughput cost
+ External Grid Interface import cost
+ carbon cost
+ unserved energy penalty
+ dumping or curtailment penalty

Core decision variables include new capacity by candidate, hourly dispatch by candidate, hourly storage charge, discharge and state of charge, External Grid Interface import, unserved energy and dumped solar or wind energy.

Single-node balance:

delivered_supply[t] + external_grid_import[t] + unserved[t]
= external_demand[t] + storage_charge[t] + parasitic_load_from_installed_assets

Renewable availability balance:

available_solar_at_node[t] = solar_dispatch[t] + solar_dump[t]
available_wind_at_node[t]  = wind_dispatch[t]  + wind_dump[t]

Storage state equation:

soc[t] = soc[t-1] + eta_charge * charge[t] - discharge[t] / eta_discharge

v1.01 dispatch tie-breaker. The linear programme can otherwise have multiple equally optimal dispatches in fixed user-system cases, especially with cyclic storage and a flat unserved-energy penalty. v1.01 adds a tiny non-commercial state-of-charge tie-breaker inside the solver objective so that, when reliability cost is otherwise equivalent, the selected solution prefers using available storage before carrying unused state of charge into shortfall hours. This tie-breaker is excluded from reported system cost, LCOE, finance outputs and cash-cost diagnostics.

10. Constraints currently supported
  • Hourly supply-demand balance.
  • Hourly renewable availability and dumping balance.
  • Candidate minimum and maximum new-build bounds.
  • Existing capacity treatment.
  • Firm generation availability limits.
  • Storage power charge and discharge limits.
  • Storage energy state-of-charge limit.
  • External Grid Interface import limit.
  • Annual minimum solar share of served demand.
  • Annual minimum wind share of served demand.
  • Annual minimum combined solar plus wind share of served demand.
  • Maximum hourly unserved power.
  • Maximum annual unserved energy.
  • Maximum annual emissions.

Share constraints are framed against served demand at the supply-demand node so storage cycling does not inflate the denominator.

9. Decision-use outputs and governance framing

v1.05 keeps the results area framed around decisions rather than metrics, preserves v1.04 pre-solve checks, and adds the Network skeleton without changing solve logic. The Decision Summary panel states the feasibility condition, primary cost driver, binding constraints and economic-fragility flags in plain language.

The binding-constraint panel deliberately avoids dual-value language. It reports whether build limits, minimum build requirements, firm availability, storage power and energy limits, grid import caps, emissions caps, unserved-energy caps or renewable-share requirements are binding or near-binding.

The commercial-reality panel translates reliability and firming into economic terms: Value of Lost Load exposure, firming insurance cost, External Grid Interface reliance and conditional LCOE. This is intended for investment-committee style discussion, not only engineering review.

The visible model-boundary panel is part of the governance design. It states that the model does not answer questions about network security, revenue sufficiency, price formation, tail risk or investment approval.

The stress-test layer is opt-in. It reruns the same linear model with simple shocks to demand, renewable output, storage efficiency, firm availability, carbon price and network assumptions, then reports cost, reliability and emissions deltas without overwriting the base solution. Stress progress and elapsed time are mirrored in the top header status bar, which is split into a right-hand status cell to reduce vertical space. Solved stress rows can be plotted in the Operational view result selector or from the stress-results table. The results area keeps cash-cost diagnostics: a capped operational time-series view of hourly marginal cash cost and hourly average cash cost for the selected plotted result, summary averages for the full year and selected range, plus a single-hour merit-order cost curve with the average cash cost and the marginal cash cost shown as dotted horizontal lines.

12. Results, Levelised Cost of Electricity bridge and operational charts
  • KPI tiles: solve status, annual cost, Levelised Cost of Electricity, emissions, unserved energy and dumped power.
  • Capacity and energy bars: high-level technology-group summaries.
  • System-wide Levelised Cost of Electricity bridge: separates renewable, firm, storage, import, carbon, reliability and curtailment cost drivers.
  • Operational view: day-time slicer, persistent bottom slicer, stack-order controls, demand-framing chart, supply-demand stack chart, dumping and charging chart, storage state-of-charge chart, hourly cash-cost chart and single-hour merit-order cost curve. Dense operational charts are capped to 720 rendered hours, equal to 30 days, while demand-framing traces and annual calculations can still use the full 8,760-hour year.
  • Detailed tables: capacity, annual energy, energy-balance audit and optimal-versus-user-system comparison.

The Levelised Cost of Electricity denominator is served demand in MWh, not gross generation or storage discharge.

13. Energy balance audit and validation logic
  • Maximum hourly supply-demand residual.
  • Maximum solar and wind availability residuals.
  • Maximum storage state-of-charge residual.
  • Maximum simultaneous storage charge and discharge.
  • Annual simultaneous storage charge and discharge overlap.
  • Maximum surplus-accounting residual.
  • Overlap between shortfall and surplus sinks.
  • Total dumped MWh per year and maximum instantaneous dump MW.
  • Timestamp of maximum dump and peak demand where relevant.

Small residuals are expected from linear-programme solve tolerance and rounding. Material residuals indicate a formulation, restoration or export issue.

14. Save, restore and export features
  • Save setup JSON: exports settings, profiles, candidate assets, demand-stack state, map/resource assumptions, constraints and plotting preferences.
  • Save setup + results JSON: also includes latest optimised and user-system results.
  • Load saved setup: restores a saved project JSON.
  • Load example setup: restores the preloaded JSON case embedded in the HTML.
  • Export dispatch CSV: exports hourly dispatch, demand, parasitic load, node demand, renewable dispatch, firm dispatch, storage charge/discharge/state of charge, External Grid Interface import, unserved energy, dumping, availability, modelled loss and residuals. Cash-cost and merit-order charts are diagnostic visualisations and do not alter the dispatch CSV schema.
  • Export finance CSV: exports the finance summary, system-wide P&L and discounted annual cash-flow table for the currently plotted solved result.
15. State object and key data structures
  • state.profiles.demand: active model demand in MW.
  • state.profiles.solar and state.profiles.wind: current default renewable profiles.
  • state.resourceProfiles.solar and state.resourceProfiles.wind: uploaded renewable profile libraries.
  • state.candidates.solar, state.candidates.wind, state.candidates.firm, state.candidates.bat: candidate definitions.
  • state.demandLibrary: PLEXOS metadata, selected region and reconstructed/scaled demand state.
  • state.loadBuilder: load components, archetype shapes, seasonal scalars and normalisation setting.
  • state.results: latest optimised result.
  • state.userResults: latest fixed user-system dispatch result.
  • state.userSystem: candidate-level fixed installed capacity entered by the user.
  • state.stackOrder: visual stack order only. It does not affect optimisation.
16. JavaScript function map for future developers

Utility and formatting

$, n, fnum, crf, escHtml, cloneObj, downloadText, parseCsvNumbers, fmtDate, hourDate.

Demand and profiles

PLEXOS reconstruction, profile scaling, Load Builder rendering, Demand Stack calculation, CSV parsing and profile previews. Preserve local-year alignment when changing these functions.

Candidate assets

Initialisation, rendering, resource-profile selectors, Min build MW, Tech. Life and conversion from UI values to model-ready candidate rows.

Optimisation

inputs() gathers UI and state. The linear-programme builder constructs variables, objective coefficients and constraints. HiGHS execution and result parsing convert variables into hourly arrays and summary metrics.

Results and charts

render(), renderOperationalCharts(), drawDispatch(), drawDump(), drawSoc(), drawAxes(), showChartTooltip(), renderComparison() and Levelised Cost of Electricity rendering functions populate the Results pane.

Persistence

Save and restore functions serialise setup state and optional results. Update these functions whenever a new persistent field is added.

17. Known limitations and technical debt
  • Network tab and candidate node assignment are active for the current up-to-four-node directed transport formulation. Security constraints and alternating-current power flow remain out of scope.
  • No hydro-specific technology class. Pumped hydro is represented through the Energy Storage Systems candidate structure.
  • No mixed-integer optimisation, unit commitment, start costs or minimum stable generation.
  • No direct authenticated in-browser Renewables.ninja fetch.
  • Embedded PLEXOS demand is compressed, not a raw byte-for-byte copy.
  • External solver and map dependencies require internet access unless vendored locally.
  • v1.03 retains hardened solver loading by trying GitHub Pages, jsDelivr and unpkg paths for HiGHS JavaScript/WebAssembly before reporting a diagnostic failure. This improves mobile and corporate-network resilience but still requires that the browser allow WebAssembly, web workers and external CDN downloads.
  • The file has accumulated iterative patch functions. If the tool becomes a maintained product, consolidate duplicate overrides or split into modules.
  • Feasibility diagnostics should be improved.
  • Tooltip and chart hit-testing are basic nearest-hour logic.
  • The embedded licence and attribution text is a publication aid, not legal advice.
18. Development rules for future ChatGPT sessions
  • Patch the latest uploaded HTML file. Do not rebuild from scratch unless explicitly asked.
  • Preserve the single-file, no-local-Python design unless the packaging strategy changes.
  • Keep the formulation linear unless there is a deliberate move to mixed-integer optimisation.
  • When adding UI fields, ensure every referenced element ID exists and persistent fields are wired into save/load.
  • When changing costs, preserve the Economic Investment Horizon and Tech. Life treatment.
  • When adding or changing candidate fields, preserve Min build MW, Max build MW, Existing MW and candidate-level user-system dispatch.
  • Keep all full-year demand and resource traces aligned to 1 Jan 00:00 local time unless explicitly changed.
  • Do not embed API tokens or credentials.
  • Run a JavaScript syntax check after edits by extracting inline script and parsing it with Node.
  • When modifying the optimiser, inspect the audit logic to confirm balance residuals remain acceptable.
19. Finance layer: discounted cash flow and P&L

The finance layer is a post-processing view for the currently plotted solved result. It does not alter dispatch, capacity expansion, constraints or the solver objective.

  • Cost of Capital / Target IRR is the target project IRR and the discount rate used in the cash-flow bridge.
  • Tax rate is applied to positive EBIT only. No tax-loss carry-forward or tax credits are modelled.
  • Depreciation is straight-line over the Economic Investment Horizon and is treated as a non-cash expense that reduces taxable income.
  • Finance LCOE is solved so annual revenue from served energy produces an IRR equal to the target cost of capital after operating costs and tax.
  • The view is screening-grade and excludes debt sculpting, depreciation schedules beyond straight line, working capital, tax losses, salvage value and detailed refurbishment timing.
20. Suggested roadmap
  • Richer candidate-level user-system comparison reporting.
  • Hydro candidate with run-of-river profile first, then reservoir logic later.
  • Multi-node transport model with linear transmission links, losses and flow limits.
  • Bidirectional link convenience mode using forward flow >= 0, reverse flow >= 0, forward flow + reverse flow <= total transfer capacity, shared build capacity and shared build cost.
  • Future bidirectional link convenience mode: one user-facing link internally creates forward and reverse non-negative flow variables, uses shared build capacity and shared build cost, and enforces forward flow + reverse flow less than or equal to total transfer capacity. This stays linear and should include a simultaneous counter-flow diagnostic.
  • Demand-side flexibility and deferrable load blocks.
  • Scenario manager and side-by-side saved case comparison.
  • Operational chart filtering by node for multi-node cases, starting with the Supply-demand stack chart.
  • Local vendoring of solver and map dependencies for offline use.
  • Better infeasibility diagnosis and constraint relaxation suggestions.
  • Benchmark cases against PyPSA, NREL, HOMER-style examples or manually verified small systems.
21. Copy-paste prompt for a new ChatGPT development window

Upload this HTML file in a new ChatGPT window and paste the following prompt.

Continue development from the latest single-file HTML. Preserve browser-only architecture, linear optimisation, GPL-3.0-or-later licensing and PLEXOS-World attribution. Patch incrementally and validate with syntax checks plus the release regression list.

Decision Summary

Decision framing converts the solved optimisation into plain-language conditions for success. It is generated from the current solved case and should be read before the detailed metrics.

Run optimisation to generate the decision summary.

Binding and near-binding constraints

This panel explains why the solution looks the way it does using simple constraint diagnostics, not dual values.

Constraint areaIndicatorExtentInterpretation
Not run--Run optimisation to populate this panel.

Commercial reality

Reliability and firming are economic choices. This panel translates the solved technical system into board-level commercial signals.

Not run
-
Run optimisation to populate this panel.

Stress test layer

Stresses are not scenarios. They are simple shocks used to see what breaks. Each selected stress reruns the same linear model from the current setup and reports deltas versus the solved base case. v1.09 added link-capacity derates, link-loss increases, selected-node demand uplifts and resource-node renewable haircuts; v1.10 added the node-link Dashboard diagram; v1.12 adds storage-location audit diagnostics for supply-node versus demand-node storage tests. v1.13 adds a node filter for the Supply-demand stack chart. Hotfix: node filter is re-applied after saved setup + results JSON loads.

Stress tests run full-year reruns one at a time. If a stress does not return within the wall-clock timeout, that row is marked as timed out and the next selected stress continues. Increase these values for slower browsers or heavier candidate sets.

Run the base optimisation first. Stress results do not overwrite the base solution.
StressStatusPlotCost deltaConditional LCOE deltaUnserved deltaEmissions deltaInterpretation
Model boundary and limitations

This visible boundary is intentional. It reduces misuse risk and keeps the tool honest.

  • Network congestion, voltage limits, inertia, frequency security or alternating-current power flow.
  • Unit commitment, minimum stable generation, starts, ramping limits or integer build decisions.
  • Market price formation, contract revenue, merchant risk or revenue sufficiency.
  • Extreme-weather tail risk, outage coincidence or probabilistic reliability.
  • Investment approval. Outputs are screening-grade evidence, not a bankable system design.

Installed capacity by technology

Annual energy by function

System cost bridge

This is a cost bridge, not a tariff, market price or investment answer. The conditional LCOE is annual all-in system cost divided by annual served demand for this feasible modelled system. It excludes risk, optionality, network security and revenue sufficiency.

Run the optimiser to populate the cost bridge. Denominator is served demand in MWh per year, not gross generation or Energy Storage System discharge. Treat conditional LCOE as one diagnostic, not the decision.

Cost by system function

Detailed cost components

Hourly dispatch explorer

Use the day-time slicer, or the persistent bottom slicer, to inspect full-day or custom hourly slices of the selected solved result. Demand-framing traces can show the full selected period, while dense operational charts are capped to a 30-day rendered window for browser performance. Chart X axes use timestamps from the model start date. Energy Storage System charging is shown below zero. Dumping means renewable energy available in that hour that is neither served nor stored.

Hourly balance: demand equals dispatched supply plus Energy Storage System discharge plus External Grid Interface plus unserved energy minus Energy Storage System charging. Renewable availability is also balanced: available solar equals dispatched solar plus dumped solar, and available wind equals dispatched wind plus dumped wind.

Demand being framed before optimisation

This chart is available before optimisation. It shows demand from load objects, reference CSV and PLEXOS-World sources against active model demand. Node demand is summarised below.

No demand profile has been framed yet.

This changes only the operational charts below. Stress-test rows become available here after successful stress solves. Selecting User-entered system will solve fixed user-system dispatch first if needed, then plot it. KPI tiles and the main capacity and energy tables continue to show the optimised base system.

Day-time slicerSelected period: full year
Day 1Day 183Day 365
Use full selected days for normal review. Switch to custom hourly window to start within a day and inspect a precise number of hours, for example 6:00 for 18 hours. All charts, legends, tooltips and the persistent bottom slicer follow the selected result and window.

Allowed keys: solar, wind, dis, firm, grid, unserved. In node-filtered charts, delivered imports follow the grid position. This changes only the visual stacking order, not the optimisation result.

Supply-demand stack chart

Stacked supply shows how solar, wind, firm generation, Energy Storage System discharge, External Grid Interface imports and any unserved energy meet node demand over the selected period. Energy Storage System charging is shown below zero because it is load on the system.

The chart legend is generated from active candidate-level series in the selected time window. In multi-node cases, the node filter can show the system total or a selected node. Drag the legend box to reposition it.

Dumped power and Energy Storage System charging

Positive values show dumped renewable power. Negative values show Energy Storage System charging, which absorbs surplus before dumping if it is economic and capacity is available.

Energy Storage System state of charge

Hourly marginal and average cash cost

Shows the selected plotted result: optimised system, user-entered system or plotted stress case. Marginal cost is a short-run cash-cost proxy from the highest-cost dispatched block in each hour, with unserved energy setting the Value of Lost Load when present. Average cash cost is hourly variable, import, carbon, unserved and dumping cash cost divided by served node demand. It is not a market price and it excludes capacity recovery. Summary figures below show full-year and selected-range averages. Click or tap a point on this chart to set the merit-order hour below.

The merit order is a cash-cost stack for the selected hour. It uses available candidate capacity in that hour, not a network dispatch curve. Storage is shown at its direct variable cost only; its opportunity value is not calculated without solver dual prices.

Merit order cost curve for selected hour

Run or plot a solved result to populate the merit-order chart.

Finance detail: discounted cash flow and system-wide P&L

Post-processing finance view for the currently plotted result. The finance LCOE is solved so constant annual revenue from served energy produces a project IRR equal to the Cost of Capital / Target IRR, after fixed costs, variable costs, tax cash expense and straight-line depreciation over the Economic Investment Horizon. It does not change the optimisation.
